title: Japanese Tamagoyaki (Rolled Omelette)

subs.name: no direct substitute — this dish is egg; paneer bhurji is a thematically different dish

ingredients.name: sugar (or mirin if available)
visual_checks: mirin gives a more rounded sweetness; plain sugar is a workable substitute
ingredients.notes: mirin gives a more rounded sweetness; plain sugar is a workable substitute

subs.name: 1 tbsp mirin if available, for a rounder, less sharp sweetness
a pinch of salt for a plainer, cream-coloured tamago without dark streaks

ingredients.name: water
visual_checks: makes the egg softer and more delicate
ingredients.notes: makes the egg softer and more delicate

subs.name: a very light coating of butter for a richer flavour

steps.text: Crack eggs into a bowl and add sugar, soy sauce and water. Stir gently with chopsticks or a fork — the goal is to combine the yolks and whites without incorporating air or making foam. The mixture should be uniform in colour but not frothy.

Place a non-stick pan or tawa over medium-low heat and add a thin film of oil, spreading it with folded kitchen paper. Pour in roughly one-third of the egg mixture — just enough to coat the base in a thin, even layer.

Let the egg set at the edges while the centre is still slightly wet, about 30 to 45 seconds. Using a spatula, roll the egg from the far end of the pan toward you in two or three folds, forming a loose log at the near edge. Do not wait for the egg to be fully firm before rolling — soft is correct.

Slide the log to the far end of the pan. Add a small drop of oil to the exposed surface, then pour in the second third of egg mixture, gently lifting the existing roll so the new liquid egg flows underneath it. Cook until the surface is barely set, then roll the existing log back over the new layer.

Repeat with the remaining egg mixture, rolling the growing log one more time to incorporate it. The finished roll should be plump, with visible white and golden layers when cut. Press gently in a bamboo mat or wrap in cling film and let it rest for 1 minute to compact the shape.
Slice the roll crosswise into pieces about 2 cm thick. The interior should show distinct layers. Arrange on a plate and garnish with spring onion. Serve warm or at room temperature.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
critical_checks: Use pasteurised eggs for preparations served raw or lightly cooked, especially for higher-risk people.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: sugar → 1 tbsp mirin if available, for a rounder, less sharp sweetness; soy-sauce → a pinch of salt for a plainer, cream-coloured tamago without dark streaks; egg → no direct substitute — this dish is egg; paneer bhurji is a thematically different dish; oil → a very light coating of butter for a richer flavour (changes allergen to include dairy)
indian_kitchen_adaptation: A non-stick tawa or small frying pan works perfectly — no special tamagoyaki pan is needed. Cook on medium-low flame so each layer sets gently without browning too quickly. The rolled technique is similar to making a thin roti-style omelette and rolling it — Indian cooks will find the motion intuitive. Soy sauce is available at most modern supermarkets and online.
